SimiCviz: Visualization of SimiC Outputs

Irene Marín-Goñi

11 March 2026

1 Overview

This vignette demonstrates how to use SimiCviz to visualize SimiCPipeline outputs and other gene regulatory network outputs.

2 Input data

2.1 SimiCPipeline outputs

You can read standard SimiC results from pickle and CSV files. If you followed the tutorials from SimiCPipeline you will have a directory set like:

Project/
├── inputFiles/
│   ├── TF_list.csv
│   ├── expression_matrix.pickle
│   └── phenotype_annotation.txt
└── outputSimic/
    ├── figures/
    └── matrices/
        └── example1/
            ├── example1_L1_0.1_L2_0.01_simic_matrices.pickle
            ├── example1_L1_0.1_L2_0.01_simic_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle
            ├── example1_L1_0.1_L2_0.01_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle
            └── example1_L1_0.1_L2_0.01_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC_collected.csv

2.1.1 Standard Output Directory

SimiCviz provides the following functions to import SimiCPipeline outputs easily.

library(SimiCviz)
simic <- load_SimiCPipeline(project_dir = "/home/workdir/SimiCviz/inst/extdata",
                               run_name = "example1",
                               lambda1 = 0.01,
                               lambda2 = 0.001)
## Found weights file: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/inst/extdata/outputSimic/matrices/example1/example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_simic_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle
## Found collected AUC CSV file: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/inst/extdata/outputSimic/matrices/example1/example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC_collected.csv
## Looking for cell labels in input files...
## Found cell labels file: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/inst/extdata/inputFiles/treatment_annotation.csv
## Cell labels file contains extra columns
## Cell / category / label / final_annotation_functional
## `weights` provided as a list.
## AUC in wide format (cells x TFs).
## No `label_names` provided; using default 'Label i' naming.
## No `colors` provided; using internal default palette.

message("\nSetting lables and colors")
## 
## Setting lables and colors
simic <- setLabelNames(simic, label_names  = c("control","PD-L1","DAC","Combo"),
              colors = c("#e0e0e0", "#a8c8ff", "#ffb6b6", "#c1a9e0"))
simic
## An object of class SimiCvizExperiment
##  4 label(s), 100 TF(s), 1000 target(s)
##  Weights: 4 matrices [0: 100 x 1000, 1: 100 x 1000, 2: 100 x 1000, 3: 100 x 1000]
##  AUC: collected (21490 cells x 100 TFs)
##  Cell labels: 21490 cells across 4 label(s) [0, 1, 2, 3]
##  Label names: 0 = control, 1 = PD-L1, 2 = DAC, 3 = Combo
##  Colors: 0 = #e0e0e0, 1 = #a8c8ff, 2 = #ffb6b6, 3 = #c1a9e0
##  TFs: Pms1, Tshz1, Wdhd1, Rere, Tead4, Mecom, ...
##  Targets: Malat1, Rn18s-rs5, Cp, Brinp3, Cmss1, Pcdh7, ...
##  Meta keys: adjusted_r_squared, run_name, lambda1, lambda2

2.1.2 Manual Loading

Alternativelly, you can load the your simic outputs manually and directly construct a SimiCvizExperiment object. #### Weights matrices

# Weights output dictionary from SimiCPipeline
weights_file <- system.file("extdata",file.path("outputSimic/matrices/example1",
                  "example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_simic_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle"), 
                            package = "SimiCviz")
# This function extracts only the weights matrices from the pickle file, 
weights_results <- read_weights_pickle(weights_file)
head(weights_results[[1]][,1:6])
##         Malat1 Rn18s-rs5         Cp Brinp3    Cmss1
## Pms1  0.000000  0.000000  0.0000000      0 0.000000
## Tshz1 0.000000  0.000000 -0.9195772      0 0.000000
## Wdhd1 0.000000  0.000000  0.0000000      0 0.000000
## Rere  1.317287  2.335413 -0.7557159      0 2.445036
## Tead4 0.000000  0.000000  0.0000000      0 0.000000
## Mecom 1.912156  0.000000  0.0000000      0 0.000000
##          Pcdh7
## Pms1  0.000000
## Tshz1 0.000000
## Wdhd1 0.000000
## Rere  0.000000
## Tead4 0.000000
## Mecom 1.331663

# If you want to access all the content use `read_pickle()` function
out <- read_pickle(weights_file)
str(out,1)
## List of 5
##  $ weight_dic        :List of 4
##  $ adjusted_r_squared:List of 4
##  $ standard_error    :List of 4
##  $ TF_ids            : chr [1:100] "Pms1" "Tshz1" "Wdhd1" "Rere" ...
##  $ query_targets     : chr [1:1000] "Malat1" "Rn18s-rs5" "Cp" "Brinp3" ...

2.1.2.1 Activity scores and cell labels

If you complete a SimiCPipeline run, you will likely have a .csv file with the activity scores for all the cells and TFs in the experiment. You can load them into the SimiCvizExperiment object to use all the plotting functionalities. Cell_labelsare required.

auc_collect_file <- system.file("extdata",file.path("outputSimic/matrices/example1"
                                ,"example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC_collected.csv"),
                                package = "SimiCviz")
auc_collect <- read.csv(auc_collect_file, header=TRUE, row.names = 1)
head(auc_collect[,1:5])
##                   Pms1     Tshz1     Wdhd1      Rere
## 01_01_28__s1 0.4016972 0.3313426 0.3129668 0.4579092
## 01_01_62__s1 0.3019129 0.3614498 0.2119206 0.5322609
## 01_02_38__s1 0.3076748 0.3899892 0.2276215 0.5210379
## 01_02_54__s1 0.5296114 0.3293531 0.4319955 0.4844924
## 01_02_81__s1 0.3219026 0.3546262 0.2260346 0.5351052
## 01_03_31__s1 0.5185712 0.2779729 0.4529818 0.4302193
##                  Tead4
## 01_01_28__s1 0.4192541
## 01_01_62__s1 0.4183672
## 01_02_38__s1 0.4482412
## 01_02_54__s1 0.4221323
## 01_02_81__s1 0.4044954
## 01_03_31__s1 0.2860450

# In New SimiCpipeline the input cell labels has "cell" and "label" columns.
cell_labels_path <- system.file("extdata",
                                file.path("inputFiles","treatment_annotation.csv"), 
                                package = "SimiCviz")
cell_labels_new = load_cell_labels(cell_labels_path, header = TRUE, sep = ",")
## Cell labels file contains extra columns
## Cell / category / label / final_annotation_functional
head(cell_labels_new)
##           cell label
## 1 19_02_08__s1     3
## 2 19_02_88__s1     3
## 3 19_03_02__s1     3
## 4 19_03_23__s1     3
## 5 19_03_58__s1     3
## 6 19_04_33__s1     3

If you have an SimiC run with the old pipeline you will have to load the activity scores matrices in pickle file and provide the cell_labels(phenotype) to the SimiCvizExperiment object to use the plotting functionalities. In this case SimiCviz will extract the cell IDs from the auc matrices and match them in order to the label phenotype

auc_file <- system.file("extdata", file.path("outputSimic/matrices/example1"
                        ,"example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle"), 
                        package = "SimiCviz")
auc_pkl <- read_auc_pickle(auc_file)

# In old SimiCpipeline the input cell labels is just a vector of labels, 
# we need to load it and convert it to a dataframe mapping "cell" and "label" columns.
cell_labels_path <- system.file("extdata", 
                               file.path("inputFiles","treatment_annotation.txt"), 
                               package = "SimiCviz")
cell_labels_old = read.csv(cell_labels_path, header = FALSE)$V1
head(cell_labels_old)
## [1] 3 3 3 3 3 3

2.1.2.2 Load SimiCvizExperiment

With these SimiCPipeline output we can generate the SimiCvizExperiment object easily.

cell_labels_path <- system.file("extdata",
                                file.path("inputFiles","treatment_annotation.csv"),
                                package = "SimiCviz")
simic2 <- SimiCvizExperiment(weights = weights_results,
                             auc = auc_collect,
                             cell_labels = cell_labels_new,
                             label_names = c("control","PD-L1","DAC","Combo"),
                             colors = c("#e0e0e0", "#a8c8ff", "#ffb6b6", "#c1a9e0"))
## `weights` provided as a list.
## AUC in wide format (cells x TFs).
simic2
## An object of class SimiCvizExperiment
##  4 label(s), 100 TF(s), 1000 target(s)
##  Weights: 4 matrices [0: 100 x 1000, 1: 100 x 1000, 2: 100 x 1000, 3: 100 x 1000]
##  AUC: collected (21490 cells x 100 TFs)
##  Cell labels: 21490 cells across 4 label(s) [0, 1, 2, 3]
##  Label names: 0 = control, 1 = PD-L1, 2 = DAC, 3 = Combo
##  Colors: 0 = #e0e0e0, 1 = #a8c8ff, 2 = #ffb6b6, 3 = #c1a9e0
##  TFs: Pms1, Tshz1, Wdhd1, Rere, Tead4, Mecom, ...
##  Targets: Malat1, Rn18s-rs5, Cp, Brinp3, Cmss1, Pcdh7, ...
# Equivalent (not run)
simic2_bis <- SimiCvizExperiment(weights = weights_results,
                             auc = auc_pkl,
                             cell_labels = cell_labels_old,
                             label_names = c("control","PD-L1","DAC","Combo"),
                             colors = c("#e0e0e0", "#a8c8ff", "#ffb6b6", "#c1a9e0"))
simic2_bis

2.1.3 Other data structures

Alternatively you can load the results from other methods in .csv format. We provide specific functions to read .csv files and convert to the expected format for SimiCvizExperiment object.

weight_path <-  system.file("extdata","example_weights.csv", package = "SimiCviz")
auc_path <-  system.file("extdata","example_auc.csv", package = "SimiCviz")
cell_labels_path <- system.file("extdata", 
                                file.path("inputFiles","treatment_annotation.csv"),
                                package = "SimiCviz")
# Loading using the paths
simic3 <- load_from_csv(weights_file = weight_path, 
                        auc_file = auc_path, 
                        cell_labels_file = cell_labels_path,
                        meta = list(lambda1 = 0.01, lambda2= 0.001))
## Cell labels file contains extra columns
## Cell / category / label / final_annotation_functional
## `weights` provided as a list.
## AUC in wide format (cells x TFs).
## No `label_names` provided; using default 'Label i' naming.
## No `colors` provided; using internal default palette.

simic3 <- setLabelNames(simic3, label_names  = c("control","PD-L1","DAC","Combo"),
                        colors = c("#e0e0e0", "#a8c8ff", "#ffb6b6", "#c1a9e0"))
simic3
## An object of class SimiCvizExperiment
##  4 label(s), 100 TF(s), 1000 target(s)
##  Weights: 4 matrices [0: 100 x 1000, 1: 100 x 1000, 2: 100 x 1000, 3: 100 x 1000]
##  AUC: collected (100 cells x 100 TFs)
##  Cell labels: 21490 cells across 4 label(s) [0, 1, 2, 3]
##  Label names: 0 = control, 1 = PD-L1, 2 = DAC, 3 = Combo
##  Colors: 0 = #e0e0e0, 1 = #a8c8ff, 2 = #ffb6b6, 3 = #c1a9e0
##  TFs: Terf1, Zfp808, Twist2, E2f7, Stat3, Pbx3, ...
##  Targets: Malat1, Rn18s-rs5, Cp, Brinp3, Cmss1, Pcdh7, ...
##  Meta keys: lambda1, lambda2

SimiCviz expects for the weights a csv with at least columns c("tf","target","weight") and for the activity scores a csv with columns c("cell", "tf","score")

# Equivalent (not run)
weight_df <- read_weights_csv(weight_path)
head(weight_df)
auc_df <- read_auc_csv(auc_path)
head(auc_df)

# Loading using the dataframe (equivalent)
simic3_bis <- SimiCvizExperiment(weights = weight_df,
                                 auc = auc_df,
                                 cell_labels = cell_labels_new, # Need to have "cell" and "label"
                                 meta = list(lambda1 = 0.01, lambda2= 0.001),
                                 label_names = c("control","PD-L1","DAC","Combo"),
                                 colors = c("#e0e0e0", "#a8c8ff", "#ffb6b6", "#c1a9e0"))
simic3_bis

3 Visualizations

Now that we have our SimiCvizExperiment object loaded with the SimiC outputs, we can use the plotting functions to visualize the results.

These functions allow you to assess the quality of the inferred GRN and visualize the TF-target relationships. First we create an output directory for the plots. You can change the path as needed.

plot_dir <- file.path(getwd(),"SimiCviz_output","plots")
dir.create(plot_dir,recursive = TRUE)
## Warning in dir.create(plot_dir, recursive = TRUE):
## '/home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots'
## already exists

4 Weights

4.1 Weight R2 assessment

To assess the goodness of the fitted model of TF-target edges we can plot the distribution adjusted R2 values across all targets.

# load_SimiCPipeline()` function will already load the `adjusted_r_squared` 
# values in the meta slot.
adjusted_r_squared <- simic@meta$adjusted_r_squared
plot_r2_distribution(adjusted_r_squared, simic, grid=c(2,2), 
                     width= 16, save =TRUE, out_dir = plot_dir)
## Saved R² distributions to: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/R2_distributions.pdf
# For manual loading you can extract the adjusted R2 values from the weights pickle file.
out <- read_pickle(weights_file)
adjusted_r_squared <- out$adjusted_r_squared
plot_r2_distribution(adjusted_r_squared, simic2_bis, grid=c(2,2),
                     width= 16,save =TRUE, out_dir = plot_dir)

Here we can see that the distribution of adjusted R2 values across all targets. Most of them are over 0.7, indicating that the model explains a significant portion of the variance in the target gene expression. However, there are also some targets with low or negative adjusted R2 values, suggesting that the model does not fit well for those targets. This could be due to various reasons such as noise in the data, missing regulatory interactions, or complex regulatory mechanisms that are not captured by the model. For these targets with low adjusted R2, we suggest to filter them out before plotting. We select a threshold of 0.7 as it was the same used to calculate the activity scores during the SimiC pipeline. You can change this threshold as needed, but you should re-run the SimiCPipeline auc calculation with the same threshold to be consistent.

unselected_targets <- list()
selected_targets <- list()
lab_keys <- names(simic@label_names)
for (lab in lab_keys){
    # Save selected for plotting
    selected_targets[[lab]] <- simic@target_ids[which(adjusted_r_squared[[lab]] >= 0.7)]
    # Save unselected for reporting
    label <- simic@label_names[[lab]]
    unselected_targets[[label]] <- simic@target_ids[which(adjusted_r_squared[[lab]] < 0.7)]
}
print("Number of unselected targets per label:")
## [1] "Number of unselected targets per label:"
print(sapply(unselected_targets, length)) 
## control   PD-L1     DAC   Combo 
##      85      91      83     165

4.2 TF Weight barplots

We will plot the top 30 targets for the top 4 TFs. You can change the number of TFs and targets to plot by changing the tf_names and top_n arguments.

 plot_tf_weights(simic,
                tf_names        = simic@tf_ids[1:4],
                top_n           = 25,
                allowed_targets = selected_targets,
                grid            = c(4,1),
                save            = TRUE,
                out_dir = plot_dir, 
                filename = "TF_weights_barplot.pdf"
                ) 
## Plotting 4 TF weight barplot(s)...
## Saved weight barplots to: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/TF_weights_barplot.pdf

4.3 Target Weight barplots

plot_target_weights(simic, 
                target_names = simic@target_ids[1:4],
                labels   = c("control","DAC"),
                save = TRUE, 
                width= 18, # Adjust width and height as needed for grid layout
                height = 10, 
                grid     = c(1, 2),
                out_dir = plot_dir, 
                filename = "Target_weights_barplot.pdf")
## Plotting 4 target weight barplot(s)...
## Saved weight barplots to: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/Target_weights_barplot.pdf

If you want to plot all TFs and Targets bar plots leave the tf_names and target_names arguments respectivelly blank.

plot_tf_weights(simic, 
                save = TRUE, 
                width= 18, height = 10,
                grid     = c(3, 2),
                out_dir = plot_dir,
                filename = "All_tf_weights_barplot.pdf")
plot_target_weights(simic, 
                save = TRUE, 
                width= 18, height = 10,
                grid     = c(3, 2),
                out_dir = plot_dir,
                filename = "All_target_weights_barplot.pdf")

4.4 Network visualization

plot_network(simic, top_n = 4)

5 Dissimilarity Analysis

To prioritize those TFs of interest, that might be driving the regulatory differences between our groups we perform a dissimilarity analysis by comparing per-cell TF activity-score distributions across phenotype labels using a histogram-based MinMax divergence.

Higher scores indicate greater regulatory dissimilarity between conditions — i.e., TFs whose activity differs most across phenotypes.

5.1 Global dissimilarity scores

dis_score <- calculate_dissimilarity(simic)
## Calculating dissimilarity scores (all cells)...
## Top 10 TFs by MinMax dissimilarity:
top_tfs <- rownames(dis_score)
##        MinMax_score
## Zfp950    0.6234324
## Nfat5     0.6198031
## Twist2    0.6087237
## Esr1      0.6077258
## Tead1     0.5861103
## Etv6      0.5815864
## Runx1     0.5729384
## Tcf7l2    0.5685862
## Arid1b    0.5685633
## Trps1     0.5587887

5.2 Dissimilarity heatmap

We can visualise the top TFs ranked by dissimilarity score in a heatmap fashion.

plot_dissimilarity_heatmap(simic, 
                           top_n = 15, 
                           save = TRUE,
                           out_dir = plot_dir,
                           cmap = "viridis",
                           filename = "dissimilarity_heatmap_top15.pdf")
## Saved dissimilarity heatmap to /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/dissimilarity_heatmap_top15.pdf

Sometimes these transcriptional dynamics are celltype or cell cluster specific. For that we might be interested in calculating the dissimilarity scores across the labels within specific cell clusters or types. We can subset the SimiCvizExperiment object by the desired labels and then calculate the dissimilarity scores and plot the heatmap as before.

# Build cell groups from metadata (e.g. Seurat clusters, cell types, etc.)
cell_groups_df = read.csv(cell_labels_path)
cell_groups  <- lapply(unique(cell_groups_df$final_annotation_functional), 
                       function(celltype) {
  cell_groups_df$Cell[cell_groups_df$final_annotation_functional == celltype]
})
names(cell_groups) <- unique(cell_groups_df$final_annotation_functional)

dissim_grouped <- calculate_dissimilarity(simic, labels = c(0,2), 
                                          cell_groups = cell_groups)
## Calculating dissimilarity for 2 cell groups...
##   Group: Proliferating cells (8328 cells)
##   Group: Basal-like (13162 cells)
## Top 10 TFs by mean dissimilarity across groups:
##        Proliferating.cells Basal.like mean_score
## Runx1            0.9569707  0.9856651  0.9713179
## Rere             0.9794053  0.9268698  0.9531375
## Zbtb20           0.9484242  0.9104576  0.9294409
## Twist2           0.8736226  0.8968298  0.8852262
## Nfia             0.7616674  0.9597451  0.8607062
## Zfpm2            0.7157497  0.9700162  0.8428829
## Etv5             0.7740717  0.8553250  0.8146983
## Stat3            0.8147071  0.7628238  0.7887655
## Bbx              0.7423388  0.8166760  0.7795074
## Zfhx3            0.8612381  0.6727203  0.7669792

plot_dissimilarity_heatmap(simic,
                            cell_groups = cell_groups, 
                            top_n = 15,
                            cmap=c("magma"),
                            save = TRUE, out_dir = plot_dir,
                            filename = "dissimilarity_heatmap_grouped.pdf")
## Saved dissimilarity heatmap to /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/dissimilarity_heatmap_grouped.pdf

You can customize the color and sorting options.

plot_dissimilarity_heatmap(simic,
                            cell_groups = cell_groups, 
                            top_n = 15, 
                            sort_by = "Proliferating.cells",
                            cmap=c("red", "white", "blue"),
                            save = FALSE)

plot_dissimilarity_heatmap(simic,
                            cell_groups = cell_groups, 
                            top_n = 15, 
                            sort_by = "mean_score",
                            cmap=c("purple"),
                            save = FALSE)

5.3 Density distributions

Plot per-TF activity score density curves across phenotype labels. These plots allow you to visually assess the differences in TF activity distributions across conditions, and can help identify TFs with distinct regulatory patterns that may be driving phenotypic differences. The fucntion allows for customization of the plot aesthetics, including fill, transparency, bandwidth adjustment for density estimation, and rug plots to show individual data points. You can also choose to save the plots directly from the function.

# Plot top 4 TFs density distributions
plot_auc_distributions(simic,
                       tf_names = top_tfs[1:4],
                       fill = TRUE,
                       alpha = .6,
                       bw_adjust = 1/8,
                       rug = TRUE,
                       save = TRUE,
                       out_dir = plot_dir,
                       filename = "AUC_distributions.pdf",
                       grid = c(2, 2))

# Plot top 6 TFs density distributions
plot_auc_distributions(simic,
                       labels = c(0,2,3),
                       tf_names = top_tfs[1:6],
                       fill = TRUE,
                       alpha = 0.9,
                       bw_adjust = 0.8,
                       rug = TRUE,
                       out_dir = plot_dir,
                       filename="AUC_distributions_filled.pdf",
                       save = TRUE,
                       grid = c(2, 3))

# Plot top 4 TFs density distributions
plot_auc_distributions(simic,
                       labels = c(0,3),
                       tf_names = top_tfs[1:2],
                       fill = FALSE,
                       bw_adjust = 0.5,
                       rug = FALSE,
                       out_dir = plot_dir,
                       filename="AUC_distributions_notfilled_multipage.pdf",
                       save = TRUE,
                       grid = c(1,2))
## Saved AUC distributions to: /home/workdir/SimiCviz/vignettes/SimiCviz_output/plots/AUC_distributions_notfilled_multipage.pdf

5.4 Cumulative distributions (ECDF)

plot_auc_cumulative(simic,
                    tf_names = c("Trps1","Zfp950"),
                    rug = TRUE,
                    grid = c(1,2),
                    save = TRUE, 
                    include_table = TRUE,
                    width = 12, height = NULL,
                    out_dir = plot_dir)

6 Trick

If you want to plot the density and cumulative distributions separatedly for different cell groups (e.g. cell types, clusters, etc.) you can subset the SimiCvizExperiment object by the desired cells. Here is how to do it:

# Build cell groups from metadata (e.g. Seurat clusters, cell types, etc.)
cell_groups_df = read.csv(cell_labels_path, header = TRUE)
celltype <- "Basal-like"
basal_cells  <- cell_groups_df$Cell[cell_groups_df$final_annotation_functional == celltype]
simic_basal <- simic
simic_basal@auc$collected <- simic@auc$collected[basal_cells, ]
simic_basal
## An object of class SimiCvizExperiment
##  4 label(s), 100 TF(s), 1000 target(s)
##  Weights: 4 matrices [0: 100 x 1000, 1: 100 x 1000, 2: 100 x 1000, 3: 100 x 1000]
##  AUC: collected (13162 cells x 100 TFs)
##  Cell labels: 21490 cells across 4 label(s) [0, 1, 2, 3]
##  Label names: 0 = control, 1 = PD-L1, 2 = DAC, 3 = Combo
##  Colors: 0 = #e0e0e0, 1 = #a8c8ff, 2 = #ffb6b6, 3 = #c1a9e0
##  TFs: Pms1, Tshz1, Wdhd1, Rere, Tead4, Mecom, ...
##  Targets: Malat1, Rn18s-rs5, Cp, Brinp3, Cmss1, Pcdh7, ...
##  Meta keys: adjusted_r_squared, run_name, lambda1, lambda2

simic_prolif <- simic
celltype <- "Proliferating cells"
prolif_cells  <- cell_groups_df$Cell[cell_groups_df$final_annotation_functional ==  celltype]
simic_prolif@auc$collected <- simic@auc$collected[prolif_cells, ]
simic_prolif
## An object of class SimiCvizExperiment
##  4 label(s), 100 TF(s), 1000 target(s)
##  Weights: 4 matrices [0: 100 x 1000, 1: 100 x 1000, 2: 100 x 1000, 3: 100 x 1000]
##  AUC: collected (8328 cells x 100 TFs)
##  Cell labels: 21490 cells across 4 label(s) [0, 1, 2, 3]
##  Label names: 0 = control, 1 = PD-L1, 2 = DAC, 3 = Combo
##  Colors: 0 = #e0e0e0, 1 = #a8c8ff, 2 = #ffb6b6, 3 = #c1a9e0
##  TFs: Pms1, Tshz1, Wdhd1, Rere, Tead4, Mecom, ...
##  Targets: Malat1, Rn18s-rs5, Cp, Brinp3, Cmss1, Pcdh7, ...
##  Meta keys: adjusted_r_squared, run_name, lambda1, lambda2
plot_auc_distributions(simic_prolif,
                       tf_names = c("Trps1","Zfp950"),
                       labels = c(0,3),
                       rug = TRUE,
                       alpha = 0.6,
                       bw_adjust = 1/8,
                       save = FALSE,
                       grid = c(1, 2))

# Plot density distributions for the basal-like cells
plot_auc_distributions(simic_basal,
                       tf_names = c("Trps1","Zfp950"),
                       labels = c(0,3),
                       rug = TRUE,
                       alpha = 0.6,
                       bw_adjust = 1/8,
                       save = FALSE,
                       grid = c(1, 2))

7 Additional functions

You can convert the activity scores file into a data.frame of cells x TFs with the following function:

auc_file <- system.file("extdata", file.path("outputSimic/matrices/example1"
                        ,"example1_L1_0.01_L2_0.001_wAUC_matrices_filtered_BIC.pickle"), 
                            package = "SimiCviz")
auc_pkl <- read_auc_pickle(auc_file)

cell_labels_path <- system.file("extdata",file.path("inputFiles","treatment_annotation.csv"), package = "SimiCviz")
cell_labels = load_cell_labels(cell_labels_path)
auc_df <- auc_list_to_df(auc_pkl, cell_labels_df = cell_labels )
head(auc_df[,1:5])

save_collected_auc(auc_df, file = "./collected_auc.csv", overwrite = FALSE)

7.1 ECDF-AUC metrics table

ecdf_metrics <- calculate_ecdf_auc(simic, tf_names = simic@tf_ids[1:6])
head(ecdf_metrics)

7.2 Summary statistics (boxplot, violin, mean bar, high-activity count)

plot_auc_summary_statistics(simic)

7.3 AUC Heatmap (mean activity per TF × phenotype)

plot_auc_heatmap(simic, top_n = 20)

If AUC data is condition-specific, you can use a heatmap:

plot_auc_heatmap(simic)

8 Exporting results

# getwd()
# out_dir <- "/home/workdir/SimiCviz/SimiCviz_output"
# dir.create(file.path(out_dir))
# export_SimiCviz_all(simic, out_dir = out_dir, prefix = "SimiC_example")

This will create:

  • data/weights, data/auc
  • plots/network, plots/auc

under SimiCviz_output/.
